story:
A tourist ship roars down into a dead city of orange polymers and crumbling concrete. The host—slick, cane in hand—unveils Watercity: once the moon’s freshwater heart, now a relic with a sagging pipeline and a glass dome rising over ruins. He promises his posh clients they are the first to walk these streets since the catastrophe and jokes about souvenirs—just not bones—before sending them off to “explore.” Camera drones hum; some guests slip into shattered buildings, others pose with bones. One man, understated in out-of-fashion black and blue, peels away without fuss. The host notices and probes. The man answers flatly that money brought him here, then lets the crowd move on.

He lingers, noticing something no one else does: a keypad glowing faintly beside a sealed door. Power, here, after two centuries. He wedges the door open with a knife and steps into stale, dusty dark. His drone’s light guides him through narrow corridors and down echoing stairs to a massive sliding door. A low hum thrums behind it. A hiss, a garbled computer voice, a wash of blinding cyan-white. As his eyes adjust, he freezes. A central machine pulses, ancient and alive. Around it: neat rows of stasis pods. Some hold skeletons suspended in brown sludge. Others flicker with green life signs. The first pod opens.

He wanders in shock, taking pictures, counting the living by their displays. A faint hello draws him to a young woman in a high-tech orange suit, skin blanched and peeling from a brutal thaw. She staggers, shivers, glares with red-rimmed eyes. She asks if he’s one of the owners. She tells him that when the moon’s minerals ran dry, their ships filled the sky—and someone poisoned the air. Many died. The rest barricaded themselves in stasis to wait for rescue that never came. He does not argue. He offers help but keeps her inside the chamber’s threshold, backing away from the plaza and speaking from the doorway while he assesses the risk. Only after sealing the upper access behind him does he race back to the ship and retrieve a secured sidearm and a quarantine kit. The host stammers when he returns in full protective gear and sees the pale survivor through the reopened seal. That’s impossible. The man’s anger is cold and focused. “It isn’t,” he says. “There are hundreds below.”

He raises the weapon—not to kill, but to enforce a perimeter. He sedates the woman quickly, apologizing, and signals the ship’s med team for immediate biohazard containment. He turns on the host. “I’m not here for entertainment. I’m a health and safety inspector.” His voice carries across the plaza, over the camera drones and the whispering tourists. “Your company brought clients into an active human site. That violates every salvage, biohazard, and heritage regulation on the books. Effective immediately, all excursions are halted. You will secure the area and await ministry responders. You will also submit your logs—purchase records, Watercity’s founding, your catastrophe files—for a full inquiry.”

The host blanches. The inspector points toward the dome, toward the keypad that still glows, toward the stairs that hide a humming machine and a long, stubborn hope. “This is not an apocalypse experience,” he says. “It’s a rescue.”
